When analyzing people data, always anonymize before pasting into Claude. Remove names, employee IDs, and any personally identifiable information. Work with aggregated data where possible.

exercises.question: Before pasting employee data into Claude for analysis, what must you do?
options: Anonymize the data by removing names, employee IDs, and personally identifiable information
Only use data from the last 30 days
Nothing — Claude is secure enough
Get each employee's permission individually
exercises.explanation: Anonymizing people data before analysis protects employee privacy. Remove names, IDs, and anything that could identify individuals. Use aggregated data where possible. This is both an ethical obligation and often a legal requirement.
hints: What's the standard practice when sharing employee data with any external tool?

exercises.question: In attrition analysis, what does the tenure-at-departure pattern reveal?
options: When employees are most likely to leave — common at 90 days, 1 year, and 2 years, each pointing to different root causes
How quickly new hires learn their jobs
The average age of departing employees
How long the company has existed
exercises.explanation: Tenure patterns reveal different problems: 90-day departures suggest onboarding failures, 1-year departures suggest unmet expectations, and 2-year departures often indicate lack of growth opportunities. Each requires a different intervention.
hints: Why might someone leave at 3 months vs. 2 years?

exercises.question: In compensation analysis, an internal ____ concern arises when employees in the same role receive significantly different pay without clear justification.
exercises.explanation: Internal equity — paying people fairly relative to their peers in similar roles — is both a legal and retention issue. Unjustified pay gaps erode trust and increase attrition risk when employees compare notes.
hints: What's the principle that everyone doing the same job should be paid fairly relative to each other?

courses.title: Building AI Workflows
courses.description: Go beyond single prompts — chain Claude tasks together, build reusable templates, measure ROI, and create AI-powered workflows your whole team can use.

lessons.title: Chaining Tasks for Complex Work
lessons.description: Break complex projects into sequential Claude tasks where each step feeds the next.

sections.content:
Chaining Tasks for Complex Work

A single prompt handles a single task. But real work is multi-step: research leads to analysis, analysis leads to a report, the report needs a presentation version. Task chaining connects these steps so Claude's output from one step becomes input for the next.

The chain concept

Instead of one massive prompt, break your work into steps:

  1. Step 1 — Research: "Summarize these 5 articles about [topic]. Extract the key findings and data points."
  2. Step 2 — Analysis: "Using the research summary above, identify the 3 most important trends and explain why they matter for our business."
  3. Step 3 — Report: "Turn the analysis into a 1-page executive brief with recommendations."
  4. Step 4 — Presentation: "Create 5 slide outlines from the executive brief, with speaker notes."

Why chaining works better than one big prompt

  • Each step produces better output when focused on one task
  • You can review and correct between steps
  • If one step is wrong, you only redo that step, not everything
  • You build a library of reusable chains for recurring work

In-conversation chaining

The simplest form: do everything in one Claude conversation. Each response becomes context for the next prompt. This works well for chains up to 4-5 steps.

exercises.question: Why does task chaining produce better results than one massive prompt?
options: It uses fewer tokens overall
Claude works faster with shorter prompts
Claude's memory is unlimited in chained conversations
Each step is focused on one task, you can review between steps, and if one step fails you only redo that step
exercises.explanation: Chaining improves quality through focus (one task at a time), control (review between steps), and efficiency (redo only the broken step). A single massive prompt forces Claude to juggle too many objectives at once.
hints: Think about why assembly lines work better than one person doing everything.

exercises.question: What is the simplest form of task chaining with Claude?
options: Opening multiple browser tabs with different Claude conversations
Doing all steps in one conversation where each response becomes context for the next prompt
Writing a Python script to automate the chain
Using Claude's API to connect multiple models
exercises.explanation: In-conversation chaining is the simplest approach: ask Claude for step 1, review the output, then ask for step 2 using that output as context. No coding required — just sequential prompts in one conversation.
hints: What's the approach that requires zero technical setup?

exercises.question: Task chaining lets you build a library of reusable ____ for recurring work.
exercises.explanation: Once you build a chain that works (e.g., research → analysis → report → presentation), you can reuse it every time you need that type of output. This turns one-time efficiency into permanent time savings.
hints: What do you call a sequence of steps you can repeat?

lessons.title: Building Reusable Templates
lessons.description: Create prompt templates your team can use consistently for recurring tasks like reports, reviews, and communications.

sections.content:
Building Reusable Templates

Every time someone on your team writes a fresh prompt for the same recurring task, they're reinventing the wheel. Templates standardize quality and save everyone time.

Anatomy of a good template

A reusable template has three parts:

  1. Context block: Background that rarely changes (company info, brand voice, output format)
  2. Variable block: Placeholders for the specific input ([CLIENT NAME], [QUARTER], [DATA])
  3. Quality criteria: What "good" looks like for this output

Example: Monthly report template

CONTEXT:
You are writing a monthly performance report for [COMPANY].
Our reporting format includes: executive summary, key metrics with MoM comparison, wins, misses, and next month's priorities.
Tone: data-driven but readable. Write for a VP audience.

VARIABLES:
Month: [MONTH]
Key metrics: [PASTE METRICS]
Notable events: [LIST ANY EVENTS]

QUALITY CRITERIA:
- Executive summary is 3 sentences max
- Every claim has a number behind it
- Misses include a root cause and action plan
- Priorities for next month are specific and measurable

Sharing templates with your team

Store templates in a shared document (Google Doc, Notion, wiki). Include usage instructions, example outputs, and tips for customization. The best templates are ones anyone on the team can use without training.

exercises.question: What are the three parts of a good reusable prompt template?
options: Context block, variable block, and quality criteria
Question, answer, and follow-up
Header, content, and footer
Introduction, body, and conclusion
exercises.explanation: The three parts serve distinct purposes: the context block provides stable background, the variable block has placeholders for each use, and quality criteria define what 'good output' looks like. This structure ensures consistent results.
hints: Think about what changes each time vs. what stays the same.

exercises.question: What should you include when sharing templates with your team?
options: A legal disclaimer about AI usage
A recording of yourself using it
Usage instructions, example outputs, and tips for customization
Just the template text and nothing else
exercises.explanation: Templates are most useful when accompanied by usage instructions (when and how to use it), example outputs (what good looks like), and customization tips. This lets anyone on the team use the template effectively without training.
hints: What would a new team member need to use the template on their first try?

exercises.question: The context block of a template contains background that rarely changes, like company info, brand voice, and output ____.
exercises.explanation: The output format (report structure, slide layout, email template) is stable context that shouldn't need re-specifying each time. Including it in the context block ensures consistent formatting across all uses.
hints: What else besides content stays consistent across uses of the same template?

lessons.title: Quality Control and Verification
lessons.description: Build review steps into your AI workflows to catch errors, verify accuracy, and maintain quality standards.

sections.content:
Quality Control and Verification

AI speed is only valuable if the output is accurate. Building verification steps into your workflows prevents embarrassing errors and builds trust in AI-assisted work.

The reviewer prompt

After Claude produces output, use a separate prompt to review it:

Review the following [report/email/analysis] for:
1. Factual accuracy — are all numbers and claims correct based on the source data?
2. Logical consistency — does the argument flow? Are there contradictions?
3. Completeness — is anything missing that the audience would expect?
4. Tone — does it match the intended audience and purpose?
5. Actionability — are recommendations specific enough to act on?

List any issues found and suggest specific fixes.

[paste the output to review]

The "red team" prompt

For high-stakes outputs (board presentations, public communications, legal documents), ask Claude to poke holes in its own work:

Act as a skeptical reviewer. Your job is to find problems with this [document]. What would a hostile audience question? What data is missing? What assumptions are unsupported? Where could someone reasonably disagree?

Spot-checking

For data-heavy outputs, verify a random sample of facts and calculations. If 3 out of 3 spot checks pass, the output is likely reliable. If any fail, review everything.

exercises.question: What is a 'red team' prompt used for?
options: Asking a different AI to review Claude's work
Asking Claude to format output in red text
Reporting errors to Anthropic
Asking Claude to find problems, question assumptions, and identify weaknesses in its own output
exercises.explanation: Red teaming asks Claude to act as a skeptical reviewer, finding holes in arguments, missing data, unsupported assumptions, and points of disagreement. It's most valuable for high-stakes outputs like board presentations.
hints: In security, what does a 'red team' do?

exercises.question: What's the recommended approach for spot-checking data-heavy AI outputs?
options: Only check the first and last items
Verify a random sample of facts and calculations — if any fail, review everything
Trust the output entirely if it looks reasonable
Spot-checking is unnecessary for AI outputs
exercises.explanation: Random spot-checking is an efficient verification strategy. If sample checks all pass, the output is likely reliable. If any fail, it signals systematic issues requiring a full review.
hints: How do quality control inspectors handle large batches?

exercises.question: For high-stakes outputs like board presentations, use a "____" prompt to have Claude poke holes in its own work.
exercises.explanation: Red team prompts force Claude to switch from 'helper' to 'critic' mode, catching issues that the initial creation prompt missed. This is especially important for outputs where errors have significant consequences.
hints: What's the security term for a team whose job is to find vulnerabilities?

lessons.title: Measuring ROI of AI Workflows
lessons.description: Track time savings, quality improvements, and cost reductions to build the business case for AI adoption.

sections.content:
Measuring ROI of AI Workflows

"It saves time" isn't enough to justify AI adoption to leadership. You need numbers. Here's how to build a compelling ROI case for Claude-powered workflows.

The time audit

Before implementing AI workflows, measure your baseline:

  • How long does this task take today? (track for 2 weeks)
  • How often is it performed? (weekly, monthly, per project)
  • Who performs it? (their hourly cost)
  • What is the quality of the current output?

After implementing the AI workflow, measure the same things. The ROI formula:

Time Saved = (Old Time - New Time) x Frequency x Hourly Cost
Quality Value = Reduction in errors x Cost per error
Total ROI = Time Saved + Quality Value - AI Tool Cost

Building the business case

Ask Claude to help build the case:

Build a business case for AI adoption using these measurements:
- Tasks automated: [list with time savings per task]
- Team size: [number of people affected]
- Current tool costs: [what we pay now]
- Proposed AI tool cost: [Claude subscription]
- Quality improvements: [error reduction, consistency gains]

Format as a one-page executive brief with a clear ROI number.

Beyond time savings

Some ROI is harder to quantify but equally real: faster speed-to-market, better employee satisfaction (less tedious work), improved consistency across outputs, and the ability to do work that was previously impossible due to time constraints.

exercises.question: What should you measure BEFORE implementing AI workflows to calculate ROI?
options: The company's total revenue
How much the AI tool costs
The baseline: how long the task takes today, how often it's performed, who does it, and current quality
How many people in the company have heard of Claude
exercises.explanation: Without a baseline measurement, you can't prove improvement. Track task duration, frequency, performer cost, and quality for at least 2 weeks before implementing the AI workflow.
hints: How do you prove something got better if you don't know where it started?

exercises.question: What intangible benefits of AI workflows should you include in a business case even if they're hard to quantify?
options: Social media bragging rights
Faster speed-to-market, better employee satisfaction, improved consistency, and ability to do previously impossible work
Reduced office space needs
The fun factor of using new technology
exercises.explanation: Beyond direct time savings, AI workflows deliver strategic benefits: getting to market faster, freeing employees from tedious work, consistent quality, and unlocking work that was previously impractical. These often matter more than raw time savings.
hints: What benefits matter to a CEO beyond just saving hours?

exercises.question: Total ROI = Time Saved + Quality Value - AI Tool ____.
exercises.explanation: The ROI formula subtracts the cost of the AI tool from the combined value of time savings and quality improvements. This gives leadership a clear net number to evaluate the investment.
hints: What do you subtract from benefits to get net return?

lessons.title: Rolling Out AI to Your Team
lessons.description: Plan an AI adoption rollout — from pilot programs to full team adoption, handling resistance, and building internal champions.

sections.content:
Rolling Out AI to Your Team

The hardest part of AI adoption isn't the technology — it's the people. Even when Claude saves hours per week, teams resist change. Here's how to run a rollout that sticks.

Start with a pilot

Don't roll out to everyone at once. Pick 3-5 team members who are:

  • Open to trying new tools (not necessarily the most technical)
  • Working on tasks where AI has clear, measurable impact
  • Willing to share their experience with the broader team

Run the pilot for 2-4 weeks. Document time savings, quality improvements, and any issues.

Address the fear

The unspoken concern: "Will AI take my job?" Address this directly. AI handles the tedious parts of work (first drafts, data cleaning, formatting). Your team's judgment, relationships, and expertise become MORE valuable when they're not buried in grunt work.

Build internal champions

Your pilot users become your champions. Have them present their wins (with specific numbers) to the broader team. Peer recommendations are 10x more persuasive than management directives.

Create a shared resource library

Build a shared folder with:

  • Approved templates for common tasks
  • Best practices and tips from the pilot
  • A FAQ addressing common concerns
  • Example outputs showing before/after quality

exercises.question: Why should you start an AI rollout with a small pilot group instead of the entire team?
options: Because most of the team isn't smart enough to use AI
To document time savings and quality improvements, fix issues, and build internal champions who can advocate to the broader team
To see if anyone gets fired from using AI
To save money on AI subscriptions
exercises.explanation: Pilots let you prove ROI with real numbers, work out issues in a controlled setting, and build advocates who can champion the tool to peers — which is far more persuasive than a top-down mandate.
hints: How do successful products typically launch — to everyone at once, or in phases?

exercises.question: How should you address team members' fear that AI will replace their jobs?
options: Promise no one will lose their job (even if you're not sure)
Ignore it — the fear will go away on its own
Only roll out AI to people who aren't worried about it
Address it directly — AI handles tedious work while their judgment, relationships, and expertise become more valuable
exercises.explanation: Addressing the fear head-on with a clear message — AI handles grunt work, freeing you for higher-value work that requires human judgment — is more effective than avoidance. People need to see how AI makes their role better, not obsolete.
hints: What's the most effective way to handle an unspoken concern?

exercises.question: Peer recommendations from pilot users are ____ times more persuasive than management directives when rolling out new tools.
exercises.explanation: When a colleague says 'this tool saved me 3 hours last week on the quarterly report,' it's far more convincing than a management email saying 'please use this new AI tool.' Pilot users become your most effective evangelists.
hints: The lesson content mentioned a specific multiplier for peer vs. management persuasion.
